intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users find recipe app friction unbearable; reducing interaction cost per ingredient via animated carousel feels lighter than traditional lists
facade.axisTargeted: animated fluid transitions vs static instant UI
facade.label: Ingredient slides in
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward motion-based UI that makes adding/browsing ingredients feel effortless and alive rather than like checklist labor
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers snappy, instant, non-animated interaction; motion may feel slow or distracting when cooking time is tight

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer clean, uncluttered interfaces that feel approachable rather than overwhelming
facade.label: Bright and minimal
facade.acceptImplies: Lean toward light backgrounds, generous whitespace, few visual elements per screen, high contrast text, feels spacious and calm
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer warmer tones, darker interfaces, or more visual richness and personality in the design

evidence.hypothesis: Users find recipe app friction unbearable; reducing interaction cost per ingredient via animated carousel feels lighter than traditional lists
evidence.implication: User gravitates toward motion-based UI that makes adding/browsing ingredients feel effortless and alive rather than like checklist labor

facade.hypothesis: For recipe-haters, a single scrolling feed of recipes (like social media) feels more casual and less intimidating than a grid or tabbed navigation system
facade.axisTargeted: List vs. Grid vs. Tabs — information architecture for recipe discovery
facade.label: One big list
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers low-friction, social-media-like browsing; linear scroll over structured navigation; feels more like casual discovery than methodical search
facade.rejectImplies: User wants clear categories, filtering, or structured navigation; finds endless scrolling chaotic; prefers tabs or sidebar to organize recipes by type/difficulty

facade.hypothesis: Users building a 'hate cooking' app might respond to voice that's irreverent and joke-forward rather than earnest—humor as permission to feel frustrated.
facade.label: Sarcastic relief
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into dark humor, self-aware messaging, and a 'I get it, cooking sucks' tone throughout UX copy and brand voice
facade.rejectImplies: Avoid sarcasm; explore earnest encouragement, practical optimism, or neutral-professional tone instead

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for cooking-haters should feel deliberately imperfect and human—like a friend's scribbled notes—rather than clinical or polished. This reduces cooking intimidation by suggesting 'you don't need to be perfect either.'
facade.label: Handwritten messiness
facade.acceptImplies: The mood leans toward approachable, forgiving, anti-perfectionist. Users want permission to be casual. Future probes: sketch-style illustrations, informal tone, relaxed layouts.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer clean, structured, trustworthy presentation. They want clarity over coziness. Future probes: crisp typography, organized grids, polished photography.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer minimal text upfront—just what they need, displayed as a scannable list rather than narrative prose
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (ingredient tables/lists) vs. key metrics (just the essentials)
facade.label: Simple ingredient list
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app should lead with a clean, itemized checklist of ingredients. No flavor descriptions or backstory. Numbers and names only.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want richer context—ingredient origins, substitution notes, or narrative descriptions that build confidence before they start.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum filtering control (dietary, time, skill level)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (quick animations, immediate feedback) vs. no-nonsense, get-out-of-the-way interactions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration

draft.title: No-Fuss Recipes
draft.summary: A recipe app designed for people who want delicious meals without the cooking complexity. Minimal ingredients, maximum flavor, zero pretension.
draft.html:
No-Fuss Recipes

Delicious meals. Zero complexity.

Quick Wins

Garlic Butter Pasta

15 min 3 ingredients

Sheet Pan Chicken

25 min 5 ingredients

Avocado Toast

5 min 4 ingredients
Filters

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking need immediate tactile confirmation that their action registered; instant visual/haptic response reduces cognitive load and anxiety about whether they've successfully completed a step
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — instantaneous micro-feedback vs. delayed response
facade.label: Instant tap feedback
facade.acceptImplies: User values snappy, reassuring interactions over smooth animations; they want to feel in control and know their action stuck immediately
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers fluid, continuous motion over staccato feedback; they may find instant pings jarring or prefer natural transitions that feel less mechanical

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum filtering control (dietary, time, skill level)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (quick animations, immediate feedback) vs. no-nonsense, get-out-of-the-way interactions
